---
title: "new"
output: html_document
date: "2026-03-17"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.



```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(GGally)
#install.packages("FNN")
library(FNN)
library(pROC)
library(caret)
```



```{r}
# Load required libraries
library(tidyverse)  # For data manipulation and visualization
library(caret)      # For model training and evaluation
library(class)      # For KNN implementation
library(ROCR)       # For performance evaluation of classification models

# Load the dataset
data <- read.csv("student_productivity.csv")

# Check the first few rows of the dataset
head(data)

# --- Step 1: Simple Linear Regression ---
# We are trying to predict 'productivity_score' based on a single feature: 'study_hours_per_day'
model_simple <- lm(productivity_score ~ study_hours_per_day, data = data)
summary(model_simple)

# Predictions
predictions_simple <- predict(model_simple, newdata = data)

# Plotting Simple Linear Regression
ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hours_per_day, y = productivity_score)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "blue") +
  labs(title = "Simple Linear Regression: Study Hours vs Productivity Score")

# --- Step 2: Multiple Linear Regression ---
# Now we include more features like 'study_hours_per_day', 'focus_score', 'attendance_percentage'
model_multiple <- lm(productivity_score ~ study_hours_per_day + focus_score + attendance_percentage, data = data)
summary(model_multiple)

# Predictions
predictions_multiple <- predict(model_multiple, newdata = data)

# Plotting Multiple Linear Regression (Only first two predictors for clarity)
ggplot(data, aes(x = study_hours_per_day, y = productivity_score)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = focus_score)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Multiple Linear Regression: Study Hours vs Productivity Score")

# --- Step 3: K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) ---
# We will use the 'focus_score' and 'study_hours_per_day' to predict 'productivity_score'
set.seed(42)
train_indices <- sample(1:nrow(data), size = 0.8 * nrow(data))
train_data <- data[train_indices, ]
test_data <- data[-train_indices, ]

# Normalizing data for KNN
train_data_norm <- as.data.frame(scale(train_data %>% select(study_hours_per_day, focus_score)))
test_data_norm <- as.data.frame(scale(test_data %>% select(study_hours_per_day, focus_score)))

# 将预测值和实际值转换为因子
knn_pred <- factor(knn_pred)
test_data$productivity_score <- factor(test_data$productivity_score)

# 确保它们具有相同的水平
levels(knn_pred) <- levels(test_data$productivity_score)

# 计算混淆矩阵
confusionMatrix(knn_pred, test_data$productivity_score)

# --- Step 4: Logistic Regression ---
# We need a binary outcome for logistic regression. We'll create a binary variable for 'high_productivity'
data$high_productivity <- ifelse(data$productivity_score > median(data$productivity_score), 1, 0)

# Logistic regression model
model_logistic <- glm(high_productivity ~ study_hours_per_day + focus_score + attendance_percentage, data = data, family = binomial)
summary(model_logistic)

# Predictions for logistic regression
predictions_logistic <- predict(model_logistic, newdata = data, type = "response")

# Convert probabilities to class labels
predicted_classes <- ifelse(predictions_logistic > 0.5, 1, 0)

# Evaluate the model using ROC Curve
pred <- prediction(predictions_logistic, data$high_productivity)
perf <- performance(pred, measure = "tpr", x.measure = "fpr")
plot(perf, col = "blue", main = "ROC Curve for Logistic Regression")

# --- Summary ---
# We have implemented 4 models:
# 1. Simple Linear Regression
# 2. Multiple Linear Regression
# 3. K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
# 4. Logistic Regression
```
